Les ressources et les subventions du secteur public devraient aller en priorité personnes qui vivent dans la pauvreté, aux populations insuffisamment desservies et aux catégories de population à faible revenu.
items_en: 91. There was an urgent need for greater political will from all Governments and commitment to mobilizing international assistance, as agreed in Cairo, to accelerate the implementation of the Programme of Action, which, in turn, would contribute to advancing broader population and development goals.

92. All developed countries are urged to make a stronger commitment to the goals and objectives of the Programme of Action, in particular with regard to the estimated costs, and to make every effort to mobilize the estimated financial resources necessary for its implementation, which have agreed, in so doing, to give priority to the needs of the least developed countries.

93. All developing countries and countries with economies in transition are urged to make a stronger commitment to the goals of the Programme of Action, particularly with regard to estimated costs, and to continue their efforts to mobilize their resources in developing countries, developed countries and countries with economies in transition are urged to promote international cooperation and to develop technical cooperation and technology transfer through South-South cooperation in order to fully implement the Programme of Action.

94. Donor countries and international financial institutions are urged to include South-South elements in development cooperation programmes and projects in order to promote their cost-effectiveness and sustainability.

95. The financial resources provided by donors have not been commensurate with the commitments regarding the goals of the International Conference on Population and Development, and there is an urgent need for donor countries to renew and step up their efforts to meet the needs for additional external resources needed for the implementation of the components of the Programme of Action, the cost of which was estimated in 1993 United States dollars, $5.7 billion in 2000, $6.1 billion in 2005, $6.8 billion in 2010 $7.2 billion in 2015. Donor countries are also urged to substantially increase the resources allocated for official development assistance to other elements of the Programme of Action, as outlined in chapter XIII of the Programme of Action, in particular the improvement of the situation of women and their empowerment, primary and basic health care, new and persistent health challenges, such as malaria and others identified by the World Health Organization as having a major impact on health, including those that cause mortality and morbidity, primary and basic health care, as well as new and persistent health challenges, such as malaria and others identified by the World Health Organization, including those that cause the highest levels of mortality and morbidity, as well as the extent of poverty, and the extent of poverty, and the extent of poverty, as agreed, must be addressed.

96. While taking fully into account their respective competencies and mandates, legislators and other decision makers are encouraged to take steps to increase support for the goals and objectives of the Programme of Action, through the adoption of laws, advocacy, public awareness-raising and resource mobilization; and advocacy should be developed at all levels, both national and international, to ensure that resources are achieved.

97. As the HIV/AIDS pandemic has increased in scale, particular attention should be paid to the need to provide the resources requested in the Programme of Action for the Prevention of Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV in a timely manner, with particular attention to vulnerable populations, especially children and young people. All countries affected by the pandemic should continue their efforts to mobilize resources from all national sources in the fight against this scourge. The international community should assist developing countries and countries with economies in transition in their efforts. Governments and the donor community should also step up their efforts to provide resources to provide care and support to persons infected with HIV and to fund specialized preventive care.

98. The international community should provide the necessary financial and technical assistance for developing countries and countries with economies in transition that have committed themselves to achieving the goals and the Programme of Action; special attention should be paid to the needs of Africa and the least developed countries, to countries facing emergency humanitarian situations and economic and financial crises or affected by such situations, as well as to the needs of developing countries suffering from low commodity prices and those facing long-term and large-scale environmental problems.

99. Donor countries and international financial institutions, including the World Bank and regional development banks, are urged to complement, upon request, the efforts of countries to meet the urgent and growing needs for basic health care and reproductive health care, including commodities, of developing and least developed countries, which are facing increasing demand for such products and declining international assistance, and of countries with economies in transition.

100. Governments and the international community should encourage and promote the adoption of additional means and mechanisms to increase the financial resources allocated to population and development programmes, including reproductive health and sexual health programmes, to ensure their sustainability, including, as appropriate: (a) advocacy for increased funding from international financial institutions and regional banks of (b) selective pricing of services provided, subsidized sales, cost-sharing and other cost-recovery services; and (c) increased private sector participation, which should facilitate access to services and should be accompanied by adequate social protection measures to ensure that people living in poverty and other members of vulnerable groups have access to them; and the possibility of adopting coordinated and effective mechanisms to address the debt problem, including external debt relief through various measures such as debt relief or debt conversion into investment in population, health and other social sectors, in order to promote sustainable development.

101. The Governments of recipient countries are encouraged to ensure that public grants and funds and assistance received from international donors for the implementation of the goals and objectives of the Programme of Action are used to maximize the benefits derived by the poor and other vulnerable groups, including those suffering from serious reproductive health problems.

102. Using the existing coordinated mechanisms at the national level, as appropriate, donor countries, international organizations and recipient countries should continue to increase their efforts and collaboration in order to avoid duplication, identify funding and ensure that resources are used to the best of their potential.

103. Governments, in cooperation with the United Nations Population Fund, as appropriate, should endeavour to ensure that resource flows are rigorously monitored, paying particular attention to the transparency and monitoring of funds for population and reproductive health programmes provided for in the Programme of Action, and non-governmental organizations could provide the necessary information in this regard, as appropriate.

104. Countries, in particular developed countries, are urged to significantly increase voluntary contributions to the United Nations Population Fund, as well as to other relevant United Nations programmes and specialized agencies, in order to enhance their ability to assist them in furthering the goals and objectives of the Programme of Action, including reproductive health programmes.

105. The Governments of developed and developing countries are encouraged to review in detail the implementation of the 20/20 voluntary compact between donor countries and interested beneficiaries, which may be a means of mobilizing more resources for the broader goals of poverty eradication, including population and social development.

106. Governments should implement policies to promote increased access to basic health care, including quality and affordable family planning and reproductive health services; encourage effective interventions and services, including private sector services, as appropriate; set standards for the provision of services; and review import laws, regulations and policies with a view to eliminating measures that unnecessarily restrict private sector participation or even participate more; and public sector resources and subsidies should prioritize people living in poverty, underserved populations and low-income groups.
